digest.top_line: RTOG 1112: adding SBRT to sorafenib lifts mOS 15.8 vs 12.3mo (HR 0.77, 1° not met at P=.06) and mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo (HR 0.55) in LA-HCC.
digest.tldr: Hepatobiliary carries the day: RTOG 1112 is the first phase 3 to test SBRT added to systemic therapy in LA-HCC, with a strong PFS gain (HR 0.55) but an OS primary that missed (P=.06) against a now-obsolete sorafenib control. Head-neck adds a multi-society reirradiation consensus, no effect sizes.

sites.intro: First multi-society (ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO) framework for reirradiating recurrent or second-primary HNSCC within prior RT fields.

studies.name: ReCOG/ESTRO/ASTRO Reirradiation Consensus
studies.tldr: Multi-society expert consensus on reirradiation for recurrent or second-primary HNSCC in prior RT fields: selection, delineation, dose accumulation, toxicity guidance.
details: 🔍 Endorsed by ReCOG, the ESTRO Reirradiation Focus Group, and ASTRO; Lancet Oncol 2026
🔍 Scope: definitive + postoperative reRT for recurrent/second-primary HNSCC within prior RT fields
🔍 Excludes nasopharyngeal cancers (2021 recs) and rare histologies
📊 15-50% of HNSCC pts develop recurrence or a second primary; few options in prior-irradiated regions

details.text: 🔍 Recommended algorithm when disease sits in a prior RT field
subdetails: Postoperative reRT for some high-risk resected pts
Definitive-intent reRT when curative surgery not feasible
Palliative systemic therapy if neither is feasible
International 17-radiation-oncologist panel, single formal voting round
Evidence graded by Oxford CEBM level 1 (randomised) to level 4 (expert consensus)

details: 🔍 Domains covered: patient selection, imaging, target delineation, planning, dose accumulation, toxicity management
🔗 Conformal era (IMRT, VMAT, proton, SBRT) improved therapeutic ratio over 20yr; modern role still undefined
⚠️ Expert-consensus document; underlying evidence heterogeneous and low-level, no effect sizes reported in source
studies.significance: The practical value for reRT planning is the guidance on dose accumulation across the prior plan and target delineation, the two steps with the widest inter-center variation. Endorsement spans ReCOG, ESTRO, and ASTRO, so it works as a shared selection-and-replanning framework, not new efficacy data (underlying evidence is largely level 3-4).
studies.monday_clinic: In a pt with recurrent or second-primary HNSCC inside a prior RT field and no salvage-surgery option, this supports structured selection for definitive reRT; it does not extend to nasopharyngeal primaries or rare histologies, which the consensus excludes.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Expert-consensus policy review** (Lancet Oncol 2026). A core group of 6 radiation oncologists, 3 physicists, and 1 research fellow drafted; an international **17-radiation-oncologist panel** voted once per statement (agree/disagree/no opinion), the no-opinion votes kept in a fixed denominator.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Guidance targets **recurrent or second-primary HNSCC arising within a previously irradiated region**, for definitive or postoperative reRT. **Nasopharyngeal cancers** (covered by 2021 recs) **and rare histologies are excluded**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: No clinical endpoint. Statement strength reported as **percentage agreement** (high ≥85%, moderate 70-84%, low <70%); evidence descriptors adapted from **Oxford CEBM level 1 (randomised) to 4 (expert consensus)**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents recurrent or second-primary HNSCC arising within a previously irradiated region. Does not represent nasopharyngeal cancers or rare histologies, which are excluded.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Frames a shared clinical framework across **patient selection, imaging, target delineation, planning, dose accumulation, and toxicity management** because reRT evidence remains heterogeneous and the modality's modern role is **still undefined**. Aim is more consistent practice and cross-center communication.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Expert consensus, not new trial data**; single voting round with no iterative re-vote. Underlying evidence is largely level 3-4, and no comparative effect sizes or survival numbers are reported in source.

open_questions: Role of modern reRT vs palliative systemic therapy undefined
Standardizing dose-accumulation methods across prior RT plans

sites.intro: SBRT enters the first-line locally advanced HCC conversation via a randomised phase 3.

studies.name: NRG Oncology/RTOG 1112
studies.tldr: mOS 15.8 vs 12.3mo adding SBRT to sorafenib (HR 0.77, 1-sided P=.06, primary not met); mPFS 9.2 vs 5.5mo, HR 0.55.

details.text: 📊 SBRT + sorafenib vs sorafenib alone, primary + key secondary endpoints

details: 🔍 First phase 3 RCT of SBRT added to systemic therapy in locally advanced HCC
🔍 SBRT personalized 27.5-50 Gy in 5 fractions, delivered before sorafenib
🔍 Eligible: HCC unsuitable for or refractory to local-regional therapy, first-line systemic candidates
⚠️ Accrual stopped early after first-line SOC systemic therapy changed → underpowered
⚠️ Prespecified OS primary formally negative (1-sided P=.06); significant only after stratification adjustment
⚠️ Sorafenib-alone control now obsolete; current 1L SOC is atezo/bev then durva+treme
📊 QoL improved at 6mo in 35% (6/17) SBRT vs 10% (2/20) sorafenib, small N
📊 Treatment-related deaths: 1 SBRT+sorafenib (lung infection) vs 2 sorafenib (liver failure, NOS)

verdict.rationale: Randomized phase 3; prespecified OS primary missed (1-sided P=.06, stopped early) but PFS positive and adjusted OS positive; contests systemic-only HCC, though sorafenib control now obsolete.
verdict.audience: Locally advanced HCC, macrovascular invasion, first-line systemic candidates

studies.significance: The RT read is a large PFS gain (9.2 vs 5.5mo, HR 0.55) plus a clinically meaningful OS signal (adjusted HR 0.72) with no added grade 3+ toxicity over sorafenib alone (47 vs 42%). Moves the add-SBRT decision in macrovascular-invasion HCC, though the sorafenib control predates the atezo/bev era.
studies.monday_clinic: In locally advanced HCC with macrovascular invasion, unsuitable for or refractory to local-regional therapy, this supports adding SBRT to systemic therapy; it does not extend to pts on modern atezo/bev or durva+treme first-line backbones.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **Phase 3** open-label RCT, 1:1, **193 randomized (177 eligible)**, multicenter (2013-2021). **Accrual stopped early** after first-line SOC systemic therapy changed.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Locally advanced HCC **unsuitable for or refractory to local-regional therapy**, candidates for first-line systemic therapy. **74% macrovascular invasion**, 150/177 male, median age 66.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Personalized **SBRT 27.5-50 Gy in 5 fractions**, delivered before sorafenib. Both arms received sorafenib.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **Primary: overall survival.** Secondary: PFS, adverse events, quality of life.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Primary OS **missed formal significance** (1-sided P=.06); PFS strongly positive and adjusted OS positive. See results table.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Grade 3+ treatment-related AEs **47% vs 42%** (P=.52), **no added high-grade toxicity** from SBRT. Treatment-related deaths: 1 SBRT arm (lung infection), 2 sorafenib (liver failure, NOS).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: **First phase 3 RCT** of SBRT added to systemic therapy in locally advanced HCC. Sorafenib-alone control **predates current 1L SOC** (atezo/bev, durva+treme).

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents locally advanced HCC with macrovascular invasion, unsuitable for or refractory to local-regional therapy. Does not represent pts on modern IO first-line backbones or resectable/transplant-eligible disease.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Early accrual halt** left the trial underpowered and the prespecified OS primary formally negative. **Obsolete sorafenib comparator**; QoL assessed in small subsets (n=17-20).

open_questions: Does SBRT add benefit to modern IO first-line backbones (atezo/bev)?
Optimal SBRT dose/fractionation in macrovascular-invasion HCC

papers.title: Stereotactic Body Radiotherapy vs Sorafenib Alone in Hepatocellular Carcinoma: The NRG Oncology/RTOG 1112 Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial.
papers.abstract:
IMPORTANCE: Most patients with locally advanced hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) recur within the liver following systemic therapy.

OBJECTIVE: To determine whether stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) improves outcomes in patients with locally advanced HCC compared with sorafenib alone.

DESIGN, SETTING, AND PARTICIPANTS: This multicenter phase 3 randomized clinical trial randomized patients with HCC 1:1 to sorafenib or SBRT followed by sorafenib, stratified by performance status, liver function, degree of metastases, and macrovascular invasion. Eligible patients had HCC unsuitable for or refractory to standard local-regional therapies and were candidates for first-line systemic therapy. Data were collected from April 2013 to March 2021, and data were analyzed from July 2022 to August 2023.

INTERVENTION: Personalized SBRT, 27.5 to 50 Gy in 5 fractions.

MAIN OUTCOMES AND MEASURES: The primary end point was overall survival (OS). Secondary end points were progression-free survival (PFS), adverse events, and quality of life.

RESULTS: Of 193 patients randomized, 177 were eligible. Accrual was stopped early due to a change in standard-of-care systemic therapy. Of 177 included patients, 150 (84.7%) were male, and the median (IQR) age was 66 (60-72) years. Macrovascular invasion was seen in 131 (74.0%). As of July 1, 2022, the median OS was 12.3 months (90% CI, 10.6-14.3) with sorafenib vs 15.8 months (90% CI, 11.4-19.2) following SBRT and sorafenib (hazard ratio [HR], 0.77; 90% CI, 0.59-1.01; 1-sided P = .06). Adjusting for stratification factors, OS was improved with SBRT (HR, 0.72; 95% CI, 0.52-0.99; 2-sided P = .04). Median PFS was improved from 5.5 months (95% CI, 3.4-6.3) with sorafenib to 9.2 months (95% CI, 7.5-11.9) with SBRT and sorafenib (HR, 0.55; 95% CI, 0.40-0.75; 2-sided P < .001). Treatment-related grade 3 or higher adverse events were seen in 37 of 88 (42%) and 39 of 83 (47%) of patients treated with sorafenib vs SBRT and sorafenib, respectively (P = .52). There were 2 treatment-related deaths in the sorafenib group (death not otherwise specified and liver failure) and 1 in the SBRT and sorafenib group (lung infection). At 6 months, improved quality of life was seen in 2 of 20 (10%) and 6 of 17 (35%) of patients treated with sorafenib and SBRT and sorafenib, respectively.

CONCLUSIONS AND RELEVANCE: In this phase 3 randomized clinical trial, among patients with locally advanced HCC, SBRT was associated with a clinically important but not statistically significant improved overall survival compared with sorafenib alone.

TRIAL REGISTRATION: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT01730937.

papers.title: Reirradiation for recurrent head and neck squamous cell carcinoma: international expert consensus recommendations endorsed by the Reirradiation Collaborative Group, the European Society for Radiotherapy and Oncology Reirradiation Focus Group, and the American Society for Radiation Oncology
